nodes.text: Eight

He walked to Lena's at seven-fifty. The town at the hour was in the gray-blue going to dark. The streetlights had come on. The rain had thinned to the kind of rain a person walks in without getting wet.
He walked Hemlock. He walked past the bookstore. He turned into the alley.

nodes.caption: Hemlock at night. The bookstore. The alley. Smolvud, Saturday.

nodes.text: Three people were at the entrance — then more. Lena was there. Wren was there. Jameson was there. Hannes was beside Jameson. Behind them, at the entrance, was Cale.
Cale had come down from ChillWave at ten to. He was in the wax canvas coat. He had two coffees from the Daily Grind in his hands and was passing them around to Wren and Jameson.
Kai.
Lena.
Everybody's here.
He looked at her.
Petra's coming at eight-fifteen. Aud said she might come if she could close the diner up early.

Finn is — Finn is at his apartment with the crow. He said he wanted to be at his apartment tonight. He said the crow was working something out. He said he would be here tomorrow morning at the bakery.
Tem and Marina are at the cabin. Tem texted ten minutes ago to say they would be at the cabin all night and would come down in the morning.
Per is at Hans's with Marit. Cale will go back to the cabin tonight with you and me.
All right.
Cale. We are at the entrance until nine.
Yeah.
She turned to Jameson and Hannes and Wren.
You three. You are at the alley. You are here because you came to the wall today and because the wall asked you to come back. You are — you are at the room with us, by Kai's invitation, by mine.

We are going to stand at the entrance and look at the face for an hour. We are not going to walk to the wall. We are not going to touch the wall. We are going to stand at the entrance. We are going to look.
We are going to do this for an hour. If you have questions, ask them after. We will answer what we can.
If you have a quiet thing happen to you in the hour — if you see something, if you feel something, if the face does something — you tell us after. Not during. We are at the entrance. We are quiet.
Lena.
Yeah.
What kind of something might happen.
Jameson. I do not know. The face has been doing things for two days. The face might do something. The face might not.
The hour is — the hour is for paying attention. Pay attention.
Yeah.
I — Lena, I am scared.
Wren.
Yeah.
You are right to be. Being scared is the right body for this. You can stand at the entrance scared.
Standing at the entrance scared is what we are doing. The rest of us are scared too. Stand with us.
Okay.
They stood at the entrance from eight to nine.

The alley at the hour was in the dim. The streetlight at the entrance threw a square of yellow into the asphalt at their feet. The wall at the far end was visible by the back-door light Petra had turned on at seven-thirty so the alley would not be dark for the people Lena had told her were coming.
The face on the wall in the back-door light was at rest — closed eyelids closed in the way Kai had registered at four forty-five. The hair held the light.
Nobody, for the hour, spoke. The rain on the asphalt was steady. The streetlight at the entrance buzzed at a small interval.
Wren stood between Lena and Cale. Jameson and Hannes stood on the other side of Kai. The eight people who had committed to the hour were at the entrance the hour. They looked.
At eight-thirty-seven, by Kai's count, a figure appeared at the far corner of the alley.
The figure was at the corner where the figure had been Friday morning. The figure was in the yellow slicker. The hood was up.
The figure was not, this time, looking at the wall. The figure was looking at the entrance of the alley. The figure was looking at the eight of them.
Wren, beside Lena, made a small sound. Lena's hand found Wren's shoulder.
The figure did not move. The eight at the entrance did not move.
The figure stood at the corner of the alley for what Kai counted as twenty seconds. The figure turned. The figure walked around the corner. The figure walked into the brick. The figure was gone.
What.
Hannes. Quiet. We are looking at the wall.
Yeah.
They stood for the remaining minutes.
At nine the face on the wall — by what Kai could see in the back-door light — did one thing. The closed lids of the closed eyes shifted by what Kai counted as the smallest possible amount.
The face — by what Kai could read — acknowledged. The face had acknowledged the eight at the entrance the way the face had acknowledged the figure when the figure had been at the corner.
The face had been at the wall the hour with them. The face had been with them.
Okay.
The eight at the entrance broke.
Wren turned to Lena and put her face in Lena's shoulder. Lena held her. Jameson and Hannes looked at each other and did not, for a count, say anything. Cale picked at the cuticle of his thumb.
We'll tell you the rest. Not tonight. Tomorrow afternoon. Come to Lena's apartment at three. Bring whoever you want to bring. We'll be there. We'll tell you what we've been figuring out.
Morning we're at Hans's. Afternoon at Lena's. You'll know everything we know by the end of the day tomorrow. You're in now.
Kai.
Yeah.
I don't have words yet.
That's all right. Tomorrow at three.
I'll bring my sister. She's been at Margit's three times this year for the dye.
Bring her.
I have to be home.
Yeah. Cale will walk you.
I will.
He took the empty coffee cups from the asphalt where they had been set. He walked Wren to the end of Hemlock toward the apartments by the river. The two of them disappeared into the dim.
Jameson and Hannes left.
Kai and Lena were the two of them at the alley entrance.
Kai.
Yeah.
She looked at us.
Yeah.
She was at the corner the way she was Friday morning. Looking at the entrance. She knew we were here.
Yeah.
Lena was quiet a count.
There are eight of us now.
Plus Cale. Nine.
Plus whoever comes tomorrow afternoon.
Yeah.
I didn't plan that. I told them to come because they came to the wall this morning. I told them to stand with us.
I didn't think — I didn't register that telling them to stand with us would put them in this. They're in by her looking at them. She looked at the eight of us and made us the eight of us.
It's going to be bigger than we thought.
Yeah.
Tem told me at the cabin Thursday night that the gathering has been waiting since 1870. She said it was the people in it. Whatever this is growing, the substrate's growing it. Bringing the town in.
Yeah.
Are you going to be okay.
I don't know. I'm tired, Kai. I haven't slept properly in five days. I'm going to be at the apartment for an hour, then drive up with you, sleep in Tem's bed, be at the bakery at six.
I'll be okay enough. I have to be okay tomorrow. I will be.
Yeah.
Thank you for coming back at eight.
Thank you for the painting.
They walked the four blocks to Lena's apartment. She packed an overnight bag in five minutes. She turned off the lights. She locked the door.

They went down to Kai's truck. The town at nine forty-something held the quiet that came after the diner crews had closed and before the late drinkers had walked home. The streetlights threw their yellow on the wet pavement. The rain had stopped briefly and started again.
They got in the truck. Kai turned the engine. He drove the road up.
The first creek crossing came at ten-fifteen. The second at ten-thirty. The Sitka stand at the top of the switchbacks stood close around the road as the headlights pushed through.
The cabin in the clearing was lit at the south window — Tem had a lamp on. The wagon was in the clearing. Marina's wagon was in the clearing. Cale's truck would be in the clearing in about an hour.
Kai parked. He turned the engine off.
He sat with Lena for a count.
Tomorrow at six. Roy. All four bowls in one room.
Yeah.
She said, after a moment:
We're it.
Yeah.
They got out of the truck. They went up the porch.
Tem opened the door before they knocked.
